questions.question: Explain the resolution principle in propositional logic with an example.
keyPoints: State that resolution operates on clauses in CNF
Explain binary resolution: if L in C1 and ¬L in C2, resolvent is (C1-{L}) ∪ (C2-{¬L})
Give example: P∨Q and ¬P∨R resolve to Q∨R
Mention derivation of empty clause indicates contradiction

questions.question: What is unification? Explain the concept of Most General Unifier (MGU).
keyPoints: Define unification as finding substitution to make literals identical
Explain MGU is the most general/substitution with no redundant bindings
MGU is unique up to variable renaming
Example: unify P(x,f(y)) and P(a,z) to get {x/a, z/f(y)}

questions.question: Describe the steps involved in converting a formula to Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF).
keyPoints: Remove implications and biconditional
Move negations inward using De Morgan's laws
Standardize apart variables (rename to avoid conflicts)
Skolemize (remove existential quantifiers)
Drop universal quantifiers
Convert to conjunction of disjunctions (clauses)
Represent as set of clauses

questions.question: What is resolution refutation? How is it used for automated theorem proving?
keyPoints: Define resolution refutation as proof by contradiction
Steps: negate theorem, add to KB, apply resolution until empty clause
Deriving empty clause proves unsatisfiability of KB∧¬theorem, hence theorem is true
Explain completeness property - if entailment holds, empty clause can be derived

questions.question: Define Horn clauses and explain their significance in logic programming.
keyPoints: Horn clause has at most one positive literal
Can be written as implication: (P1 ∧ P2 ∧ ... ∧ Pn) → Q
Definite clause (exactly one positive) and goal clause (no positive)
Important because they enable efficient backward chaining
Used in Prolog - facts are unit Horn clauses, rules are definite clauses

questions.question: Explain two resolution strategies and their properties.
keyPoints: Unit resolution: at least one parent is unit clause - complete for Horn clauses
Input resolution: at least one parent from original set - complete for ground clauses
Linear resolution: one parent is resolvent from previous step - complete
Set of support: one parent from negated theorem - reduces search space

questions.question: What is the occurs check in unification? Why is it important?
keyPoints: Occurs check prevents binding variable to term containing that variable
Prevents infinite structures during unification
Example: unifying x with f(x) would create infinite term f(f(f(...)))
Without occurs check, unification may succeed incorrectly
Modern Prolog implementations include occurs check for soundness

questions.question: Solve the following using resolution: Given KB: (1) ¬P ∨ Q, (2) ¬Q ∨ R, (3) P. Prove R using resolution refutation.
keyPoints: Negate theorem: ¬R (goal clause)
Add to KB: {¬P∨Q, ¬Q∨R, P, ¬R}
Resolve (3) P with (1) ¬P∨Q on P: resolvent = Q
Resolve Q with (2) ¬Q∨R on Q: resolvent = R
Resolve R with ¬R (from negated theorem): resolvent = {} (empty clause)
Empty clause derived ⇒ KB∧¬R unsatisfiable ⇒ R is proved
Show each resolution step clearly with complementary literals identified

questions.question: Convert the following formula to CNF: ∀x (P(x) → (∃y (Q(y) ∧ R(x,y))))
keyPoints: Step 1: Remove implication: ∀x (¬P(x) ∨ ∃y (Q(y) ∧ R(x,y)))
Step 2: Move negation inward: Already no negated quantifiers
Step 3: Standardize variables (already distinct)
Step 4: Skolemize: Replace y with Skolem function f(x) since x is bound: ∀x (¬P(x) ∨ (Q(f(x)) ∧ R(x,f(x))))
Step 5: Drop universal: (¬P(x) ∨ (Q(f(x)) ∧ R(x,f(x))))
Step 6: Distribute OR over AND: (¬P(x) ∨ Q(f(x))) ∧ (¬P(x) ∨ R(x,f(x)))
Show each transformation clearly with reasoning

questions.question: Explain the resolution algorithm for first-order predicate logic. Include discussion on unification and substitution.
keyPoints: First-order resolution extends propositional with unification

Algorithm: 1) Convert KB and negated theorem to CNF 2) Loop: select two clauses, find complementary literals, compute MGU, resolve to get new clause 3) Add resolvent to clause set 4) If empty clause derived, proof complete
Unification: find substitution making literals identical, compute MGU
Substitution: mapping from variables to terms, applied to literals/clauses
Resolution terminates when empty clause found or no more resolvents possible
Proof of completeness: Herbrand's theorem guarantees finite proof if entailment holds
Discuss complexity and strategies to reduce search space

questions.question: What is subsumption? How is it used to optimize resolution theorem proving? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: Subsumption: clause C1 subsumes C2 if ∃θ such that C1θ ⊆ C2
Example: P(x) subsumes P(a) (substitution {x/a})
Also P ∨ Q subsumes P (adding redundant literal)
Used for clause reduction: more specific clauses subsumed by general ones can be removed
Reduces search space by eliminating redundant clauses
Set of support strategy: ensures each resolution step involves negated theorem
Unit resolution strategy: restricts one parent to unit clause
These strategies trade completeness for efficiency (except specific cases)
